sentiment.rationale: Ezekiel's inaugural vision of God's throne-chariot with the four living creatures, wheels within wheels, and the glory of the Lord.
God commissions Ezekiel to speak to a rebellious house of Israel, warning they are impudent and stiffhearted.
Ezekiel eats the scroll and is appointed watchman over Israel; warned of accountability for failing to deliver God's warnings.
Symbolic siege of Jerusalem using a tile and iron pan; Ezekiel lies on his side to bear the iniquity of Israel and Judah.
God pronounces severe judgments on Jerusalem—famine, sword, pestilence, and scattering—because of their abominations.
Prophecy against the mountains of Israel; idolatry will be punished with slaughter and desolation, but a remnant will remember.
Declares the end has come upon the land; a day of wrath, violence, and the complete collapse of Israel's pride.
Vision of abominations in the temple—image of jealousy, elders burning incense to idols, women weeping for Tammuz, sun worship.
Vision of six slayers and a man with an inkhorn; the faithful are marked, the rest are slain beginning at the sanctuary.
The glory of the Lord departs from the temple threshold, carried by the cherubim and wheels—a terrible departure.
Judgment on wicked counselors in Jerusalem, but a promise of gathering and a new heart for the exiles.
Ezekiel acts out exile with his baggage; prophecy that the prince in Jerusalem will be captured and the people scattered.
Condemnation of false prophets who prophesy out of their own hearts and daub walls with untempered mortar.
God warns against idolatry of the heart; even Noah, Daniel, and Job could only save themselves by their righteousness.
Jerusalem compared to a useless vine—good for nothing except burning because of unfaithfulness.
Extended allegory of Jerusalem as an unfaithful wife rescued from birth, adorned by God, but turning to harlotry; yet covenant remembered.
Parable of two eagles and a vine representing Babylon and Egypt; Zedekiah's broken oath will bring judgment, but God will plant a tender twig.
Landmark chapter on individual accountability: the soul that sinneth shall die; the righteous shall live; God has no pleasure in death.
A lamentation for the princes of Israel—a lioness whose cubs are captured, a vine plucked up and withered.
God recounts Israel's history of rebellion from Egypt onward; despite repeated idolatry, He will gather them and purge rebels.
The sword of the Lord drawn against Jerusalem; Babylon's king divines at the crossroads; the crown is removed until Messiah comes.
Catalog of Jerusalem's sins: bloodshed, idolatry, profaning the sabbath, extortion; no one stands in the gap.
Allegory of Oholah (Samaria) and Oholibah (Jerusalem) as adulterous sisters who sought after Assyria and Babylon.
Parable of the boiling pot representing Jerusalem's judgment; Ezekiel's wife dies as a sign and he is forbidden to mourn.
Oracles of judgment against Ammon, Moab, Edom, and the Philistines for rejoicing over Jerusalem's fall.
Prophecy against Tyre: many nations will come against it, Nebuchadnezzar will besiege it, and it will become a bare rock.
A lamentation over Tyre depicted as a magnificent ship made from finest materials that sinks in the seas.
The prince of Tyre condemned for claiming to be a god; a lament over the king of Tyre cast from Eden; brief oracle against Sidon.
Prophecy against Pharaoh and Egypt as a broken reed; Egypt will be desolate forty years then restored as a lowly kingdom.
The day of the Lord against Egypt; Babylon's sword will come upon it and its allies; Pharaoh's arms will be broken.
Pharaoh compared to Assyria as a great cedar brought down to Sheol in its pride—a warning against exaltation.
Lamentation over Pharaoh as a dragon caught in a net; Egypt descends to the pit with the other fallen nations.
Ezekiel reappointed as watchman; individual accountability restated; news of Jerusalem's fall arrives; the righteous shall live.
Condemnation of Israel's false shepherds; God Himself will search for His sheep, set up one shepherd (David), and make a covenant of peace.
Prophecy against Mount Seir (Edom) for perpetual hatred and bloodshed against Israel; it will be made desolate.
Prophecy to the mountains of Israel: restoration for God's name's sake; a new heart and new spirit promised; the land will be like Eden.
Vision of the valley of dry bones brought to life; the two sticks of Judah and Joseph joined; everlasting covenant and sanctuary.
Prophecy against Gog of Magog who will invade Israel in the latter days, but God will fight with pestilence, fire, and brimstone.
Gog's armies destroyed on the mountains of Israel; seven years to burn weapons; God's glory shown among the nations.
Vision of the future temple begins: a man with a measuring reed shows Ezekiel the gates, courts, and chambers in detail.
Detailed measurements of the inner temple, the most holy place, the side chambers, and the carved decorations of cherubim and palm trees.
The priests' chambers are measured; distinction between holy and common space is established for the temple precinct.
The glory of the Lord returns to the temple from the east, filling the house; instructions for the altar of burnt offering.
The east gate sealed because the Lord entered by it; regulations for the Levites and the sons of Zadok in temple service.
The holy portion of land for priests, Levites, and prince; instructions for offerings, feasts, and the prince's responsibilities.
Regulations for the prince's worship at the gate, sabbath and new moon offerings, and the boiling places for sacrifices.
Waters flowing from the temple threshold become a river bringing life wherever they go; trees for food and healing; boundaries of the land.
Division of the land among the twelve tribes; the holy district and the city with twelve gates; the city named Jehovah-shammah (The Lord is there).

summaries.summary: Ezekiel sees a vision of four living creatures with four faces and four wings, wheels within wheels, and the likeness of the glory of the Lord above a throne of sapphire.
God commissions Ezekiel to speak to the rebellious house of Israel, commanding him not to fear, and gives him a scroll written with lamentations and mourning.
Ezekiel eats the scroll, is sent to the house of Israel, and is appointed as a watchman responsible for warning the wicked and the righteous.
Ezekiel performs symbolic acts portraying the siege of Jerusalem, lying on his left side 390 days for Israel and 40 days on his right for Judah, eating rationed food.
Ezekiel shaves his head and divides the hair into thirds to symbolize Jerusalem's fate: a third burned, a third struck by the sword, and a third scattered.
God pronounces judgment against the mountains of Israel for their high-place idolatry; the slain will fall among their idols, though a remnant will be spared to remember.
The end has come upon the four corners of the land; God's wrath brings violence, pestilence, and economic collapse; silver and gold cannot deliver in the day of wrath.
In vision, Ezekiel is taken to the temple in Jerusalem where he witnesses escalating abominations: the image of jealousy, secret idolatry, Tammuz worship, and sun worship.
Six executioners and a man with an inkhorn are sent through Jerusalem; those who sigh over abominations are marked for preservation; the rest are slain.
The cherubim and wheel-throne vision is repeated; coals of fire are scattered over Jerusalem; the glory of the Lord departs from the temple threshold to the cherubim.
Twenty-five wicked princes are condemned; Pelatiah dies; God promises the exiles a new heart and spirit, pledging to gather them and be their sanctuary.
Ezekiel digs through a wall and carries his belongings out by night as a sign of exile; the prince in Jerusalem will be captured; every vision will be fulfilled soon.
Condemnation of false prophets who see vanity and divine lies, and of prophetesses who sew pillows and make kerchiefs to hunt souls; their walls of whitewash will fall.
Elders with idols in their hearts are rebuked; even if Noah, Daniel, and Job were in the land, they could only save themselves; four sore judgments on Jerusalem.
Jerusalem is compared to a vine branch that is useless for any work—good only for fuel—because the inhabitants have trespassed grievously.
An extended allegory of Jerusalem as an abandoned infant whom God rescued, clothed, and married, but who became a harlot worse than Sodom and Samaria; yet God will remember His covenant.
A riddle of two eagles and a vine: Babylon carried away the king, Egypt could not help, and Zedekiah's broken oath will bring judgment; God will plant a noble cedar on a high mountain.
God declares each soul is accountable for its own sin; children do not bear their father's iniquity; the wicked who repent shall live; God has no pleasure in the death of the wicked.
A lamentation for the princes of Israel: two young lions taken captive (Jehoahaz and Zedekiah), and a vine (the nation) plucked up, withered, and burned.
God recounts Israel's history of rebellion from Egypt to the wilderness to Canaan; despite their idolatry He will gather them, purge the rebels, and bring them to His holy mountain.
The sword of the Lord is drawn and polished for slaughter; Babylon stands at the crossroads and chooses Jerusalem; the crown is overturned until He comes whose right it is.
A catalog of Jerusalem's sins: bloodshed, idolatry, oppression of strangers, profaning the sabbath, dishonesty, and immorality; no one is found to stand in the gap.
The allegory of two sisters, Oholah (Samaria) and Oholibah (Jerusalem), who played the harlot with Assyria and Babylon; both will drink the cup of desolation.
The parable of the boiling pot symbolizing Jerusalem's siege; Ezekiel's wife dies and he is forbidden to mourn, signifying Jerusalem's coming grief beyond mourning.
Oracles against four neighboring nations: Ammon, Moab, Edom, and Philistia—all condemned for their hostility toward Judah during its destruction.
Prophecy against Tyre: because she rejoiced over Jerusalem's fall, many nations will rise against her; Nebuchadnezzar will besiege her and she will become a bare rock.
A lamentation over Tyre depicted as a magnificent merchant ship built of the finest materials from many nations, which sinks in the heart of the seas.
The prince of Tyre is condemned for claiming to be a god; a lament over the king of Tyre recalls a figure in Eden; God also pronounces judgment on Sidon and promises to restore Israel.
Pharaoh is compared to a great dragon in the Nile; Egypt will be desolate forty years and then restored as a lowly kingdom; Babylon is given Egypt as wages.
A further oracle against Egypt: the day of the Lord brings sword upon Egypt and its allies; the arms of Pharaoh are broken and Babylon is strengthened.
Pharaoh is warned through the example of Assyria, likened to a great cedar of Lebanon that was brought down in its pride to Sheol.
A lamentation over Pharaoh as a sea monster caught in a net; Egypt descends to the pit and lies among the uncircumcised fallen nations.
Ezekiel is reappointed watchman; individual responsibility is restated; news arrives that Jerusalem has fallen; the survivors in the land are warned against presumption.
God condemns Israel's false shepherds who feed themselves instead of the flock; He will search for His sheep Himself, set up one shepherd David, and make a covenant of peace.
Prophecy against Mount Seir (Edom) for its perpetual hatred and bloodshed against Israel and its claim over the lands of Israel and Judah.
God promises to restore the mountains of Israel for His name's sake, cleanse the people, give them a new heart and a new spirit, and make the desolate land like the garden of Eden.

Ezekiel sees dry bones come to life as a great army symbolizing Israel's restoration; two sticks for Judah and Joseph are joined, signifying reunification under one shepherd with an everlasting covenant.
Prophecy against Gog of the land of Magog, who will invade Israel in the latter days with a great army, but God will intervene with pestilence, blood, fire, and brimstone.
Gog's armies are destroyed on the mountains of Israel; it takes seven months to bury the dead and seven years to burn the weapons; God pours out His spirit on Israel.
In the twenty-fifth year of exile, Ezekiel is taken in vision to a high mountain and shown a future temple; a man with a measuring reed measures the gates and outer court.
The inner temple is measured: the nave, the most holy place, the side chambers, the decorations of cherubim and palm trees, and the wooden altar.
The priests' chambers north and south of the temple are measured; they are designated as holy space where priests eat the most holy offerings and change garments.
The glory of the Lord returns to the temple from the east, filling the house with His presence; God commands the altar dimensions and the seven-day consecration ritual.
The outer east gate is shut because the Lord entered by it; regulations distinguish the faithful sons of Zadok from unfaithful Levites; priestly conduct is prescribed.
A holy portion of the land is set apart for the sanctuary, priests, Levites, and the prince; just weights and measures are required; feasts and offerings are specified.
Regulations for the prince's sabbath and new moon worship at the inner gate; instructions for daily offerings, inheritance, and the boiling kitchens for sacrifices.
Waters issue from under the temple threshold flowing eastward, deepening into a river that heals the Dead Sea and gives life; trees grow on its banks; the land boundaries are described.
The land is divided among the twelve tribes in parallel strips; the holy district with the temple is in the center; the city has twelve gates and is named The LORD Is There.

crossReferences.note: The four living creatures with multiple faces parallel the four beasts around the throne in Revelation.
Ezekiel is appointed watchman over Israel in both his original commission and recommission after Jerusalem's fall.
Individual righteous prayer and its limits—even the most righteous can only save themselves.
A tender twig planted on a high mountain, paralleling Isaiah's Branch from the root of Jesse.
Each person dies for their own sin; children shall not be put to death for their fathers.
The crown overturned until He comes whose right it is, echoing Shiloh in Jacob's blessing.
God will set up one shepherd over His flock, paralleling Christ as the Good Shepherd.
A new heart and spirit of flesh rather than stone, echoed in Paul's description of hearts as tablets of flesh.
The breath of life entering dead bodies, paralleling the two witnesses standing on their feet.
The stick of Judah and stick of Joseph becoming one, understood in LDS theology as the Bible and Book of Mormon.
God's tabernacle with His people forever, echoing the new Jerusalem where God dwells with man.
Gog and Magog appear again in Revelation's account of the final battle after the millennium.
The glory of God illuminating the land, paralleling the Lamb as the light of the New Jerusalem.
Waters flowing from the temple giving life, paralleling the river of the water of life from God's throne.
A city with twelve gates named after the tribes, paralleling the New Jerusalem in Revelation.

historicalContext.setting: Babylon (prophecy directed at the land of Israel)
Jerusalem (in vision), east gate of the temple
Babylon (valley of dry bones in vision)

verses.reason: Ezekiel sees the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the Lord and falls on his face.
God addresses Ezekiel as 'son of man'—a title used over 90 times in this book.
Ezekiel appointed watchman: if you warn the wicked, you have delivered your soul.
Declares Jerusalem set in the midst of the nations, yet she has rebelled more than them.
Silver and gold cannot deliver in the day of the wrath of the Lord.
The faithful who sigh and cry over abominations receive a mark of preservation on their foreheads.
Promise of a new heart of flesh to replace the stony heart—a key covenant promise.
False prophets condemned for daubing with untempered mortar—building false peace.
Even Noah, Daniel, and Job in the land could only deliver their own souls by righteousness.
Despite unfaithfulness, God will remember His covenant and establish an everlasting covenant.
God brings down the high tree and exalts the low—a messianic promise.
The soul that sinneth, it shall die—the landmark statement of individual accountability.
God has no pleasure in the death of him that dieth; wherefore turn and live.
God gave Israel His sabbaths as a sign between Him and them.
The crown overturned until He comes whose right it is—a messianic prophecy.
God sought for a man to stand in the gap but found none—a call to intercessory service.
The king of Tyre described as having been in Eden, the garden of God—a passage often linked to the fall of Lucifer.
God has no pleasure in the death of the wicked but that the wicked turn from his way and live.
God will set up one shepherd, His servant David, to feed the flock—a messianic prophecy.
God will make a covenant of peace and cause showers of blessing to come down in season.
A new heart and a new spirit: the stony heart removed and a heart of flesh given—central to covenant theology.
The desolate land shall become like the garden of Eden.
Prophesy upon these bones and say: O ye dry bones, hear the word of the Lord—one of the most vivid images in scripture.
The two sticks of Judah and Joseph joined into one—interpreted as Bible and Book of Mormon.
God's tabernacle with His people forever; He will be their God and they His people.
God will pour out His spirit upon the house of Israel.
The glory of the God of Israel comes from the east and His voice is like the noise of many waters.
God declares the temple the place of His throne and the place of the soles of His feet where He will dwell forever.
The east gate shall be shut because the Lord God of Israel has entered by it.
Waters issuing from under the threshold of the temple, flowing eastward, bringing life wherever they go.
Trees by the river with leaves that do not wither and fruit for food—their leaf for medicine.
The name of the city: Jehovah-shammah, 'The LORD is there'—the closing words of the book.
